tokens: Here comes an AGS CAL (calibration) at you, Houston.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: You can close your fuel vent, now.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Are you copying those torquing angles?
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Houston, did you copy the results of the AGS CAL?
(Pause while Carr finishes a brief engineering conversation with Dick Gordon)
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Did you copy the results of the AGS CAL, down there?
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: I think I did something I'd bet I'd never do.
I believe I shut that beauty off in the air before touchdown.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: No; I was on the gauges!
That was the only way I could see where I was going.
I saw that blue contact light, and shut that baby down and we zipped in from about six feet.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Boy, you can sure see the stars out of this AOT.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: (Responding to Pete's comment about the landing) Pete, the Air Force guys here say that was a typical Navy landing.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: As long as the hook was down and we didn't bolt, I'm happy.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: We'd like to check out a couple of
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: And we entered one wrong number and did a Verb 32.
Is there any way to wipe out that set of marks, now that we did a Verb 32?
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: We'll confer with the experts, here.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: I can do the program over again.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: They're asking for thirty more seconds (to consider the problem).
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: (Pause) All we want to do is get outside, that's all.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: The simplest thing to do is do the program over.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: And there are your torquing angles.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: And there's your Lat(itude), Long(itude), and altitude.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Let us look at that for a second.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Looks like Dick did some good (landmark) tracking, with an altitude like that.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: It's even fun at one-sixth g inside of this spacecraft.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: As soon as we landed, we started handling the books like we do in the simulator at one g.
We were throwing them off the table (laughing) and over near the circuit breakers and what have you.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: That's done, and we're going to P00.
(Long Pause) And if it's okay with you, we'll go ahead and do the second P57 right now.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: We're just chasing a funny (an apparent peculiarity) in the AGS.
We're convinced it's okay; and we're just trying to figure it all out.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: There are the torquing angles (from the second P57).
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: What do you want to do with that one?
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Let's look at it for just a minute, Pete.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Your first one was a little better.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: If you go P00 and Data; we're ready to fire you up some new (state) vectors.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: It looks like you better tweak up your steerable (high-gain antenna) a little bit.
It looks like we're beginning to lose a little signal.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Okay, what do you recommend as the best possible angles?
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: The yaw'll be good, but the pitch isn't even close.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: That's where we are now, Houston.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: I've got the octal data for your P22 work-around procedure.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: And I've got a DAP (Digital Autopilot) load update for LM weight for you.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Your new LM weight is 10802 (lbs for the Ascent Stage).
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: (Pause) And, Houston, are we Stay yet?
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: We'll go ahead on (that is, continue) Surface (Checklist page) 9 and start our stay partial power down.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: And do you have a P22 Acq(uisition) time?
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: It's on your lunar surface Pad.
Stand by and I'll read it out to you again.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: We have an update on the P22 Acq time for you.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: You have a new RLS (Reference Landing Site) and state vector.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: You got a bunch of happy geologists in the back room waiting to go.
Say, we're standing by with a LM consumables update and also standing by for your description (of the view out the window).
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: We were just working on that and I'm very close to where I want to be, but I'm trying to pin it down exactly.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: I guess, Houston, for planning purposes, we landed very close to the Head of the Snowman.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: We're having a little trouble judging distance.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: (Possibly reading a chart) Intrepid, your shadow length on a level surface is 250 feet.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: (Incredulous) You've got to be kidding me!
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: We could shorten that a bit to 230.
Which way do you think you are?
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Well, if my shadow's 230 feet long, we're really misjudging distances (which they are).
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Well, I'd say that my shadow was much shorter than that.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Is Intrepid going to do a P22?
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: A reminder: prior to your P22, I'd like you to execute Verb 41, Noun 72.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: (Long Pause) What Verb 41, Noun 72 do you want me to do anyhow?
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: The step you carried out on Surface 9 going to 270/180 is good.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: We'll shorten that shadow length up for you a bit.
If we assume a 3-1/2-degree slope all the way (up to the west), then you'll come up with a 150-foot shadow.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: How wide a diameter is the Head of the Snowman?
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Diameter of the Head Crater from one inside rim to the other inside rim is around 400 to 500 feet.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Right on the Head of the Snowman, to the left (south), let's use
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: The coordinates which you gave us, are those the coordinates of the crater or the coordinates of your present location?
And, also, repeat your question related to the blocky rim.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Well, I want to know if that crater that I gave you...
I think I have that crater in sight.
And it's a very blocky rim crater, and I want to know if the crater that I gave you the coordinates of is a very blocky rim crater.
I think I'm sitting right next to the head of the Snowman on the right-hand side at coordinates S...
I think that's where I landed.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: The coordinates you gave us of the crater are right next to Bench Crater.
Bench Crater being at L.5 rather than M.5.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: I got to get on this P22.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: We're standing by to track (the CSM).
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: (Still examining the map) Yeah; it's Bench Crater.
That's the one I'm referring to.
And I think I landed at Head Crater, almost...
Just a little bit past one of the traverses that you got laid out there.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: And we're trying to decide here whether your present position is really R.2 rather than S.8.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: We'll give a description here in another 15 minutes or so.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: And we're standing by with a consumables update.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Houston, tell him I'm showing him locked on.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: Intrepid has a visual on Yankee Clipper.
Relay to Yankee Clipper (that) I've got him visually.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: (Pause) Clipper, for your info(rmation), Intrepid has a visual on you.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Do you have all the data?
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: The next time, I want to see you put that Intrepid right in the middle of the sextant.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: Houston, Intrepid's ready to copy the consumables update.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: (Responding to Pete) Intrepid, consumables update for GET (1)11 plus 50: RCS A, 80 (percent remaining); B, 75: O2 descent, 87.6 and 97.4 (percent remaining); H2O, 78.7, 99.2 (percent remaining); amp hours (remaining), 1243.3, 572.3.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Okay, I got it all, except the only thing that wasn't clear was the time.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: We'll be right back with you.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: From your data, where do you think we landed?
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: (Changing the subject) We have a good E-MOD dump.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: I think it's just a matter of...
I think it's just a matter of a few hundred feet one way or another.
I flew right by the side of the (Surveyor) crater and ground her to a halt and parked it, but that's about my best guess right now where we are.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: We'll be massaging the data and be getting back to you on that.
We have the data on the E-MOD.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: On your previous question on Bench Crater, from our maps here, we can't tell whether that has a blocky rim.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Well, you can take the Sun line for a reference; (and) that crater's about 5 degrees off to the left of the Sun line of my shadow.
And it is a very blocky rim.
Depending on how far away it is, there's some blocks over there that may be 8 feet.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: You're looking at the crater which is 5 degrees south of the Sun line?
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Five degrees left of the shadow.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Soon as we get done eating here, we'll get with it.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: (Joking in response to Ed's "You deserve it.") No, they don't.
Tell them to get to work.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: For heaven's sakes, Ed, they're down there having all the fun, and you and I are doing all the work.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: They'll get with it soon, Clipper.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Help us get a better visual pin-down of where you are.
Are you able to locate a 50-foot block, approximately 100 foot (sic; means feet) right in front of you; (possibly correcting himself) or an 8- to 10-foot block about 50 feet in front of you?
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Well, I can't say that there is anything like that.
There is one great big block that looks to me like it's 1500 or 2000 feet in front of us that meets that description.
It's a really big fellow sitting out there.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Let me say this: there's another large crater (Head Crater) right smack in front of us, but it's not obvious to us.
There is no shadow length (that is, the shadow in the bottom of Head is hidden from them).
(The) Sun angle is so low that we're sitting here where we don't see any shadows; and, unless we look very carefully, it's not obvious to us that there is a big crater out there.
Now, I kind of think that that may be the Head of the Snowman that's sitting out further past me.
I'm not sure that I'm not sitting right smack on the other side (meaning the west side) of the Surveyor crater, just a little bit past it.
I think really the best thing for us to do is to get out and look around.
The sooner we do that, the quicker we'll figure out where we are.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: We concur with that, and we're also standing by for your LM description.
We have your lift-off times for Rev 16 through 19.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Al's finishing off eating and, while he does that, I've been sitting here scanning with the monocular.

And the first thing I should give you, according to the checklist here (is that), as you already know, we flew right by the side of the Snowman (meaning Surveyor Crater), and landed right past him some little bit.
And our yaw angle is 10 degrees (clockwise), and my general impression is that we're in country where I see mostly angular rocks.
(There are) very few rocks at hand that are rounded.
Now, I'll let Al talk about the close-up stuff (later).
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Intrepid, we'd like to get to Yankee Clipper before LOS, and we'll be right back with you.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Go ahead with your description of the horizon, and we'll be breaking in to you as soon as we can get back with Yankee Clipper.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: The blocky rim crater that I previously mentioned...
When I look through the monocular, everything has a pure white look; these big blocky boulders look pure white.
Now, some of them are really big; and, when I say big, I'm talking 8, 10, maybe 20 feet.
Some on the horizon (pause) got to be 20 feet across.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Your Comm is breaking up slightly.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: We were laughing about that ourselves.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: I'm back on the air again.
And just a general comment about all these blocks in the surrounding terrain: at first glance out of the spacecraft, I can distinguish absolutely no color difference in anything.
About the only difference is looking cross-Sun (north or south) versus down-Sun (west, directly away from the Sun).
I'm sure that some of these rocks have different colors and different textures; but, from here, viewing from the spacecraft, they don't appear that way.
Looking at all the materials on the horizon and the blocks on the horizon, they all appear to be of the same material, and they all appear to be pure white.

Now, we've got a pretty low Sun angle and I'm looking at them at a low angle (so that they reflect a great deal of light back toward the spacecraft), so they (may) have varying other colors; but in this monocular, they all appear white.
As I said, the size goes all the way up to, I'm guessing, 20 feet.
A couple of big ones on the horizon (appear to be that size).
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Intrepid, in order to pin down your location a little bit better, would you try to give us the location with respect to the LM - that is, distance and angle from your Z-axis - of the large block that you have on the horizon?
And also the large craters, craters that are roughly 20 feet in diameter or larger.
That is, slightly larger than the LM shadow width.
Also, we ought to press on here fairly quickly, as we are getting a little bit behind our timeline.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: (Responding to Gibson's suggestion that they press on with the site description) That's what we think, too.
I'll tell you what we're going to do.

I'll give you a good description here; and we're going to get ready; and when we get out, we'll take the TV and show you the craters; and I think you'll have a pretty good handle on it (that is, they'll have enough information to pinpoint the landing site).
Generally, right now, we're sitting on a...
(It's) not a level surface, but we don't see any particularly high hills, either.
It's just sort of like an undulating plain?
You can see quite far in all directions.
There doesn't seem to be any particularly high objects such as mountains or high hills or anything like that that interferes with the view.
The only features that are obvious, besides the just general rolling country that we're on, is the blocky rim craters that are visible in almost every direction.
Some of them are quite close, and some of them are far away.
We got one of them, for example, at 12 o'clock (that) Pete described.

(It's) about 2000 or 3000 feet away, (that is, far enough) that if it didn't have these large boulders on it and had a pretty nice raised rim - perhaps maybe even up to (a) 10-feet-high raised rim - we wouldn't be able to see it.
I guess the diameter of that crater must be on the order of 600 to 700 feet (actually about 1700 feet).

We've got a number of more weathered craters around us of every size: from one that's just outside the window here at 2 o'clock (out about) 15 feet that's about 6 feet in diameter and about 3 feet deep, all the way up to one (Middle Crescent Crater) that I see over at the 1 o'clock position.
It doesn't have a particularly raised rim, but it looks like it could have a diameter in the order of 400 feet (or maybe) 500 feet (actually about 1500 feet).
(Pause) There are many rocks that are scattered around on the surface.
Most of them are partially buried; and, as they are buried there, you can see that there are little fillets of dirt that has built up around almost all of them.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: And I can't tell, of course, if it (the filleting)'s only from this direction or not (that is, he can only see the sides of the rocks facing the LM), but all these rocks seem to have the same characteristics, whether they are small or large.

One interesting feature that is directly at our 12 o'clock, about 20 feet, is a whole surface area that's a bit different from the rest in the fact that it's got sort of parallel lines or parallel trenches or skin trenches perhaps an eighth of an inch deep and running what would be north to south to us; and you can see it from about my 2 or 3 o'clock position, all the way over to Pete's window.
Some sort of force has apparently caused these traces to be made in the surface.
I don't think it was our engine, because, as I say, they are perpendicular to the lines that our engine would have made.
We'll be able to get a better look at that when we get out, of course; and we'll also be able to use that close-up stereo camera on it and just get some good pictures of it.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: Pete also pointed out that there doesn't seem to be any possibility here of seeing anything like a contact between different-colored surfaces.

There may be a chance to notice the contacts or different materials by looking at the texture: for example, that area that I described as directly in front of the LM that has those north-south lines on them.
But, other than that, it just looks like one uniform surface with many, many craters in it.
There's no immediately-apparent white-rim craters near us.
Most of the ones that I can see out my window don't have a raised rim at all.
They don't have any particular elongation.
They seem to be - just from glancing at them - about the same texture as the areas surrounding them.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: I think you're going to like this place, though, Houston, because we can see, in the not-too-far distance, some pretty nice-size rocks that are on the edge of some craters that we suspect could be bedrock from below the regolith here.
Well, I guess we'll have to get outside and find how far we can move and how fast, so that we know which ones of these we can visit.
It looks there are going to be some good places out here to the west to put the ALSEP.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: And I think, in general, that just...
We're going to be able to gather a lot of good information from where we are.
This is a lot better surface, I think, than Pete or I had imagined before we got here.
It looks like we're going to be able to move around pretty well and it looks like there's going to be a lot of different types of samples lying about.
So I think, probably, with that we'll go ahead and start rigging out.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: Before we hustle on here, could you give us one quick answer?
What is the distance of the 400-foot crater which you see at 1 o'clock?
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: I'd say it's about 500 feet, and it runs from about my 12:30 to my 2 o'clock position.
It doesn't look like it has any particular blocks on the rim.
I think we'll be able to pinpoint ourselves pretty well when get out and look behind us a little, and maybe walk over to one of these craters.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: Also, Houston, I landed - not 20 feet behind me - if I peer around the corner of the window here, I'm right on the edge of another great big crater.
(This is, of course, Surveyor Crater.) It falls away at a, oh, I'm going to say 10-degree slope at least, right behind us.
We're right on the edge, we landed right past a fairly large crater.
I'd say 300 to 400 feet in diameter.
(Garbled) apparent to me to look for when I came in for a landing but (garbled) down (garbled) around the back.
And I was going to (garbled) to keep them close to my (garbled) back about 50 feet.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: That'll give us a lot to work with while you're in the EVA Prep.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Intrepid, that'll give us a lot to work with while you're in your EVA Prep.
We're standing by for that now.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: I guess the next thing is, I got to do P06 and Power down the IMU, if y'all concur.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Say, Al, it sounds as though your cold has cleared up considerably.
(In the background, somebody says "Don't say 'cold'") It sounds as though your stuffed head has cleared up considerably.
Could you give us the last time at which you took the Actifed?
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Took the Actifed just before we put our helmets on prior to DOI.
I don't recall exactly what that time was.
And it started clearing up along about PDI time, and I think that being in this gravity field right here is helping it a bit.
As I say, I don't have any cold; it just seems to feel a bit stuffy.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: Houston, what time are we scheduled to go out for EVA?
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: You're scheduled for Cabin Depress about 1 hour from now.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: When you're looking for them in the sextant, if you look at the Head Crater, we suspect that they are on the northwest rim of the Head Crater.
That's the head of the Snowman.
transcript.speaker: Gibson
